name: Wesna
classification_notes: Condensed letterforms with bold strokes, sharp stroke joints and distinctive features drawn from Slovenian interwar poster lettering; the strong personality and historical grounding place it firmly in display rather than text use.
summary: A condensed display typeface by Alja Herlah rooted in Slovenian interwar poster lettering, available in three weights with italics across Latin and Cyrillic.
description: Drawn from the letterings found on Slovenian posters of the interwar period, Wesna brings bold strokes, sharp stroke joints and condensed proportions into a contemporary digital typeface. Three weights from Light to Bold each carry a matching italic, with coverage spanning Latin and Cyrillic scripts. The typeface won the Brumen Biennial award for best in typography in 2024, recognising its work in preserving Slovenian typographic heritage.
foundry_blurb: Type Salon describe Wesna as a reflection of the current state of design rooted in Slovenian interwar poster lettering. The typeface combines bold strokes, condensed letterforms, sharp stroke joints and unique features to preserve Slovenian typography heritage, connecting past and present through a new digital formation. It is available in four weights with italics in both Latin and Cyrillic.

name: Sonetni Venez
classification_notes: The letterforms are drawn from the first edition of Prešeren's Sonetni venec, published in 1834 in Ilirisches Blatt, placing this firmly in the historical revival category with a serif character consistent with early 19th-century printing types.
summary: A type revival drawn from the 1834 first printing of France Prešeren's Sonetni venec, with five glyph variants per character simulating the natural variation of lead type on paper.
description: Designed for Vuk Ćosić's Nacija – Kultura project, exhibited at MG+MSUM Ljubljana, the typeface lifts its forms directly from the original 1834 newspaper printing of Prešeren's Sonetni venec. Each glyph carries five distinct variants that cycle through the line so that no two identical forms appear consecutively, capturing the impression variance of letterpress. The font was used to generate a new-age sonetni venec from Twitter posts by Slovenian politicians, tying typographic authenticity to contemporary political satire.
foundry_blurb: Type Salon describe Sonetni Venez as a typeface designed for the Nacija – Kultura project by Vuk Ćosić, exhibited at MG+MSUM Ljubljana. Its letterforms are taken from the first edition of the Sonetni venec, published in Ilirisches Blatt in 1834. Each glyph has five variants that alternate across the line so no two identical forms repeat, documenting the natural diversity of lead type impressions on paper. The typeface was used to generate a contemporary sonetni venec built from Twitter posts by Slovenian politicians.

name: Plecnik
classification_notes: Described as having classical proportions, humanist stroke endings and low contrast, with sans-serif stroke endings and geometric shapes; sits within a humanist serif tradition despite some mixed sans influences, with a classical structure rooted in Plečnik's architectural lettering.
summary: A typeface derived from the letterforms and design principles of the Slovene architect Jože Plečnik, offered in four weights with italics and a Display variant.
description: Rooted in research into Plečnik's extensive archive of inscriptions, book designs and architectural lettering, the typeface draws on classical proportions and humanist terminals with low stroke contrast. Four weights from Light to Bold each include matching italics, while a separate Display style amplifies the most distinctive features of Plečnik's hand. Suited to book and editorial design, branding and packaging where a historically grounded but contemporary serif is needed.
foundry_blurb: Type Salon describe Plecnik as following the principles of Jože Plečnik's architecture and graphic design, presenting a piece of Slovene typographic legacy. The typeface is not a direct digitalisation of his sketches but a reflection of current design trends rooted in his letter shapes, defined by classical proportions, humanist stroke endings and low contrast. It was recognised for excellent Slovenian design at the Brumen Biennial 2022.

name: Sopran
classification_notes: Sopran features long, straight serifs in place of traditional drops, high contrast distributed across weights from monolinear hairline to extreme black, placing it firmly in the display serif category with expressive, poster-oriented characteristics.
summary: A display serif by Krista Likar built around long straight serifs, extreme weight contrast and playful punctuation drawn with monolinear strokes.
description: Sopran spans five weights from Thin to Black, with contrast moving from near-monolinear at the lightest end to extreme at Black. Long straight serifs replace traditional bracketed drops and enable discretionary ligatures such as "fa" and "Ta". Symbols and punctuation are drawn with monolinear strokes to offset the drama of the letterforms, and two stylistic sets extend typesetting options for headlines, posters, signage and display applications.
foundry_blurb: Type Salon describe Sopran as a typeface whose character is defined by long serifs that replace traditional drops, enabling discretionary ligatures like "fa" and "Ta". Contrast ranges from monolinear in the hairline to extreme in the black, while symbols and punctuation are drawn with monolinear strokes for a more playful typesetting feel. Two stylistic sets broaden setting options, with the typeface suited to headlines, larger texts, show posters, displays and signage.

name: Univerza Sans
classification_notes: Described as combining Slovenian avant-garde influences with recognisable Slovenian typographic forms; its functional range across identity, wayfinding and academic use places it firmly in the humanist sans tradition.
summary: Commissioned for the centenary of the University of Ljubljana, this sans draws on Slovenian avant-garde and typographic heritage to serve a broad institutional range from wayfinding to academic publishing.
description: Designed by Alja Herlah and winner of the Brumen Biennial award for best typography in 2022, it was created specifically for the University of Ljubljana's hundredth anniversary. The family spans four weights from Light to Bold, each with an italic, giving enough range for formal correspondence, signage and scientific articles within a single coherent voice. Its Central European language support and blend of official weight with visual distinctiveness make it well suited to complex institutional identity systems.
foundry_blurb: Type Salon describe Univerza Sans as developed to mark the hundredth anniversary of the University of Ljubljana, with a style influenced by Slovenian avant-garde and recognisable forms from Slovenian typography. Intended for multifunctional use across identity systems, formal communication, wayfinding and academic publishing, it speaks Central European languages and feels official while remaining attractive and recognisable.

name: Spektra
classification_notes: A multi-script variable family with a unified counter space driving weight distribution across five scripts; the humanist sans classification reflects the organic, culturally considered approach to letterform construction across Latin, Cyrillic, Arabic, Greek and Hebrew.
summary: A variable, multi-script family by Krista Likar and Alja Herlah covering Latin, Cyrillic, Arabic, Greek and Hebrew within a single unified counter-space system.
description: Designed by Krista Likar and Alja Herlah, Spektra is built around a shared counter space that governs weight distribution across all five scripts, ensuring visual coherence without flattening the distinctive characteristics of each writing system. It operates as a variable font with weight and slant axes, running from very thin to an extreme black defined by the thickness of Arabic horizontals. Each weight comes in italic and backslant styles, making it a highly expressive choice for multilingual editorial and identity work. The family has received a GRANSHAN special mention, the Modern Cyrillic 2021 award and recognition at the Brumen biennial.
foundry_blurb: Type Salon describe Spektra as a versatile multi-script type family combining Latin, Cyrillic, Arabic, Greek and Hebrew, unified by a shared counter space that shapes the weight distribution of every style. A variable font with weight and slant axes, it pushes from the thinnest strokes to the heaviest, where the black is defined by the thickness of Arabic horizontals. Each weight includes italic and backslant variants, and the family has received awards from GRANSHAN, the Brumen biennial and Modern Cyrillic competitions.
